title: The Magicians of Caprona - Chapter 15 (Fragment 6)
text:
There were murmurs from Montanas and Petrocchis alike, here, that the quarrel was not ridiculous.

“Of course it was,” said Chrestomanci. “You’ve all been deceived from your cradles up. You’ve let Ricardo and Francesco fool you for two centuries. What they really quarreled about we shall never know, but I know they both told their families the same lies. And you have all gone on believing their lies and getting deeper and deeper divided, until the White Devil was actually able to enter Caprona again.”

Again there were murmurs. Antonio said, “The Duchess was the White Devil, but—”

“Yes,” said Chrestomanci. “And she has gone for the moment, because the words were found and the Angel awakened by members of both families. I suspect it could only have been done by Montanas and Petrocchis united. The rest of you could have sung the right words separately, until you were all blue in the face, and nothing would have happened. The Angel respects only friendship. The young ones of both families are luckily less bigoted than the rest of you. Marco and Rosa have even had the courage to fall in love and get married—”

Up till then, both families had listened—restively, it was true, because it was not pleasant to be lectured in front of a crowd of fellow citizens, not to speak of the Duke and the Archbishop—but they had listened. But at this, pandemonium broke out.

“Married!” screamed the Montanas. “She’s a Montana !” screamed the Petrocchis. Insults were yelled at Rosa and at Marco. Anyone who wished to count would have found no less than ten aunts in tears at once, and all cursing as they wept. Rosa and Marco were both white. It needed only Rinaldo to step up to Marco, glowering, and he did. “This scum,” he said to Rosa, “knocked me down and cut my head open. And you marry it!”

Chrestomanci made haste to get between Marco and Rinaldo. “I’d hoped someone would see reason,” he said to Rosa. He seemed very tired. “It had better be Venice.”

“Get out of my way!” said Rinaldo. “You double-dealing sorcerer!”

“Please move, sir,” said Marco. “I don’t need to be shielded from an idiot like him.”

“Marco,” said Chrestomanci, “have you thought what two families of powerful magicians could do to you and Rosa?”

“Of course we have!” Marco said angrily, trying to push Chrestomanci aside.

But a strange silence fell again, the silence of the Angel. The Archbishop knelt down. Awed people crowded to one side or another of the Cathedral yard. The Angel was returning. He came from far off down the Corso, on foot now, with his wing tips brushing the cobbles and the chorus of voices swelling as he approached. As he passed through the Cathedral court, it was seen that in every place where a feather had touched the stones there grew a cluster of small golden flowers. Scent gushed over everyone as the Angel drew near and halted by the Cathedral porch, towering and golden.

There he turned his remote smiling face to everyone present. His voice was like one voice singing above many. “Caprona is at peace. Keep our covenant.” At that he spread his wings, making them all dizzy with the scent. And he was next seen moving upwards, over lesser domes and greater, to take his place once more on the great dome, guarding Caprona in the years to come.

This is really the end of the story, except for one or two explanations.

Marco and Rosa had to tell their story many times, at least as often as Tonino and Angelica told theirs. Among the first people they told it to was Old Niccolo, who was lying restlessly in bed and only kept there because Elizabeth sat beside him all the time. “But I’m quite well!” he kept saying.

vocabulary.definition: A supernatural being or spirit, often a messenger of God or protector.
vocabulary.example: “And she has gone for the moment, because the words were found and the Angel awakened by members of both families.

vocabulary.definition: Filled with a feeling of respect mixed with fear or wonder.
vocabulary.example: Awed people crowded to one side or another of the Cathedral yard.

vocabulary.definition: Showing strong, unreasonable beliefs and refusing to accept different opinions.
vocabulary.example: The young ones of both families are luckily less bigoted than the rest of you.

vocabulary.definition: A group of people singing together.
vocabulary.example: He came from far off down the Corso, on foot now, with his wing tips brushing the cobbles and the chorus of voices swelling as he approached.

vocabulary.definition: A group of similar things positioned closely together.
vocabulary.example: As he passed through the Cathedral court, it was seen that in every place where a feather had touched the stones there grew a cluster of small golden flowers.

vocabulary.definition: A formal agreement or promise.
vocabulary.example: Keep our covenant.

vocabulary.definition: A baby's bed or the starting point of something.
vocabulary.example: “You’ve all been deceived from your cradles up.

vocabulary.definition: The act of using offensive words or swearing.
vocabulary.example: Anyone who wished to count would have found no less than ten aunts in tears at once, and all cursing as they wept.

vocabulary.definition: To cause someone to believe something that is not true, typically to gain an advantage.
vocabulary.example: “You’ve all been deceived from your cradles up.

vocabulary.definition: Separated into parts or groups.
vocabulary.example: And you have all gone on believing their lies and getting deeper and deeper divided, until the White Devil was actually able to enter Caprona again.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling as if everything is spinning around; disoriented.
vocabulary.example: ” At that he spread his wings, making them all dizzy with the scent.

vocabulary.definition: Rounded roofs on buildings.
vocabulary.example: And he was next seen moving upwards, over lesser domes and greater, to take his place once more on the great dome, guarding Caprona in the years to come.

vocabulary.definition: Dishonest or deceitful behavior.
vocabulary.example: “You double-dealing sorcerer!

vocabulary.definition: Looking at someone angrily or with a scowl.
vocabulary.example: It needed only Rinaldo to step up to Marco, glowering, and he did.

vocabulary.definition: Words or actions that offend or show disrespect.
vocabulary.example: Insults were yelled at Rosa and at Marco.

vocabulary.definition: Statements that are not true, told with intent to deceive.
vocabulary.example: What they really quarreled about we shall never know, but I know they both told their families the same lies.

vocabulary.definition: Low continuous sounds or voices speaking quietly.
vocabulary.example: There were murmurs from Montanas and Petrocchis alike, here, that the quarrel was not ridiculous.

vocabulary.definition: A wild uproar or chaos.
vocabulary.example: But at this, pandemonium broke out.

vocabulary.definition: An angry argument or disagreement.
vocabulary.example: There were murmurs from Montanas and Petrocchis alike, here, that the quarrel was not ridiculous.

vocabulary.definition: To admire or regard with esteem.
vocabulary.example: The Angel respects only friendship.

vocabulary.definition: Unable to rest or relax due to anxiety or boredom.
vocabulary.example: Among the first people they told it to was Old Niccolo, who was lying restlessly in bed and only kept there because Elizabeth sat beside him all the time.

vocabulary.definition: Protected from harm or danger.
vocabulary.example: “I don’t need to be shielded from an idiot like him.

vocabulary.definition: Complete absence of sound.
vocabulary.example: But a strange silence fell again, the silence of the Angel.

vocabulary.definition: A person who practices magic or witchcraft.
vocabulary.example: “You double-dealing sorcerer!

vocabulary.definition: In need of rest or sleep.
vocabulary.example: He seemed very tired.
